title: Social Relations—Restraint—Road to Freedom, Part 2
author: Alberta H. Christensen
content:
Immortality and Eternal Life
Lesson 2: Restraint—Road to Freedom, Part 
Alberta H. Christensen (Reference: Immortality and Eternal Life, Vol. 2, Selections from the Writings and Messages of President J. Reuben Clark, Jr., Melchizedek Priesthood Course of Study, 1969-70, Lessons 6 and 7.)

Northern Hemisphere: Third Meeting, November 1969
Southern Hemisphere: April 1970

To point out that understanding the difference between immutable, spiritual and physical laws and the regulations which apply to them, may influence one's attitude toward the regulations.

Our Heavenly Father, with infinite wisdom and understanding, seemingly reveals admonition and warning to his earth children when that counsel and commandment may be most needed and rewarding. How beneficial the divine counsel may be depends upon our willingness to accept and follow the revealed commandment. In lessons 6 and 7 of the current Melchizedek Priesthood course of study, President Clark points out several occasions when this divine counsel and warning have been given to us. (Other examples might be cited.) This correlating lesson considers these occasions and also distinguishes between the principle involved in the law and the rules and regulations which make possible the enforcement of the law.

Again, much will be quoted directly from the Melchizedek Priesthood course of study since President Clark's clarification of the subject is both logically and interestingly expressed.

Consider the example of ancient Israel to whom the law of the Lord was given, by way of restraint, in the affirmative commandment of the Decalogue, "Thou shalt have no other gods before me." (Exod. 20:3.) How needed this commandment was at that particular time is evident when we recall that Israel, newly emancipated from Egyptian bondage, was spiritually weak as a result of having lived for generations in an idolatrous land. The liberated people were now moving into a land (Canaan) inhabited by a people who likewise were idolators, worshipping many gods, and practicing human sacrifice. These environments were not compatible with the life the chosen people were to live if they were to carry out the work which God had planned for them. The idolatrous practices were not consistent with the building of a personal moral life nor with the building of stable homes upon which a Lesson Department righteous and sound society is dependent.

President Clark states: God's work could not then, and cannot now, be maintained in partnership with a worship which calls for the heathenish practices of human sacrifice and grossly immoral rites. Our own civilization cannot be preserved in the presence of such heathenish practices, for a moment's reflection will show how human sacrifice would outrage every feeling of humanity that generations have fostered.... In our time when the Western world has come to a worship of one God only, the need for such a commandment, as among ourselves, is no longer necessary, unless it is to be applied figuratively. (It may be parenthetically observed that such an application might not be a strained one, for modern man-chas, in a too large extent, made materialism his god.) (Immortality and Eternal Life, Vol. 2, lesson 6.)

Thus we see how important it was for this restraining commandment to be given at that particular time.

Another example of restraining counsel given in a time of special need is the law of health known to us of today as the Word of Wisdom. We discuss it here:

1. As a restraint or forewarning given at a time of special need.
2. As an example that truth, although relating to the physical body, is as unchanging—unless some other law intervenes—as the immutable laws governing other areas of the physical universe.

Although the Word of Wisdom was revealed to us before scientific research had given us the confirming facts of which we now are aware, modern science supports the claims of this law of health. An interesting point of view is clarified by President Clark:

It is demonstrated that nicotine, alcohol, tannin, and caffein exert their effects upon the body, and the Lord and modern science say these effects are harmful. These effects are visited upon us whether we wish the effects or not; whether we consent to them or disprove them. These noxious things enforce their own mandates against the body, no other agency is necessary or used. This is the law.

Some complain about the Word of Wisdom as if it were a rule of conduct imposed upon them by the Church authorities and so a rule that could be changed by the Church authorities. In accordance with this idea certain persons have suggested changes or modifications of the Word of Wisdom. But the Word of Wisdom is not a rule of conduct; it is a law—the Lord's law—of health. It was promulgated by him. The law existed before he told it to us; it would exist if the revelation were blotted out from the book. The Church authorities have nothing to do with the law. God, speaking through the forces of the physical world, has prescribed it, and so long as those forces exist, the law will remain.

It is therefore the foolish ignorance of a child to assume that the First Presidency can issue a rule that will permit the use of any of these injurious things without their harmful effects. It would be an easy and, in one sense, a pleasing gesture, as satisfying Church members who wish to use these harmful substances to declare the Word of Wisdom no longer existent. But such a declaration would be no more efficacious than a declaration that the law of gravitation no longer operates.

(Ibid., lesson 7.)

For Discussion
1. Perhaps you are one who has wished that the Word of Wisdom might be modified or withdrawn. If so, does the foregoing approach to this law of health add a new dimension to your thinking?
2. How may the attitude toward the Word of Wisdom presented by President Clark improve one's thinking relationship to the Church authorities?
3. Would young people be influenced more favorably toward acceptance of this divine admonition if they were taught from the beginning that it is not merely a restraint imposed upon them by the Church but an unchanging law of health?

It is relevant to recall that the Lord also gave a law of health to ancient Israel during the days of wandering in the wilderness.

It was a dietary and sanitary code, given through Moses, and was founded on scientific principles. One of the prohibitions of that code was that the Israelites should not eat sea foods unless they had fins and scales. President Clark states: Today we eat many wholesome seafoods that do not meet this ancient requirement. Again it must be said that it is not for me to attempt to suggest the reasons why the Lord gave this great law, nor the reasons why he made this and other dietary distinctions. But looking at the matter from the point of view of human knowledge, one reason why the Lord forbade such things to ancient Israel might have been the extreme perishability of certain sea foods which would not be found either in the wilderness or in Palestine, seafoods which rapidly decay, and which, in decaying, generate poisons destructive of health and, indeed, of life itself. Ancient Israel had no such rapid transportation as would enable the delivery of such food materials while they were still fresh, and no refrigerating processes by which they might preserve such sea food materials pending the time they were to be eaten. Under such conditions, even a wise human lawgiver would today enact such a law as was given to ancient Israel, and for the reasons I have suggested. But in our time, with our rapid transportation, our efficient refrigeration, such seafoods may be properly preserved and, so preserved, seem as wholesome as other sea foods that have fins and scales; the Lord has not forbidden these foods to us and we eat them.

(Continued)

Does this discussion by President Clark give you a different point of view as to this ancient dietary code? Discuss briefly.

By way of comparison it may be observed in passing that the difference in the character of the two codes of laws given and of the things proscribed in each is an important illustration and example of how God speaks to a people in accordance with their stage of knowledge, their culture, and their surroundings. I do not pretend to say why the Lord gave to Israel some commands and withheld others. But this much may be said. It was seemingly unnecessary to forbid the use of tobacco, tea, and coffee to ancient Israel, for apparently they knew nothing of these things; and alcoholism though forbidden to the priests under certain conditions and discountenanced to the people, had not taken hold of them. It was unnecessary in our day to give the intimate instructions of the Mosaic code concerning sanitation and diet because science was revealing to us the laws relating to such things and we were generally obeying them. But our science had not yet reached, when the Word of Wisdom was given, accepted conclusions upon drugs and narcotics, nor upon the effects of alcoholism which had become almost a besetting sin. In this situation the Lord revealed his modern law of health. So God spoke to each people in the language and terms they understood and in accordance with their needs.

Application Applying this same reasoning to teaching within the home, discuss the following questions:

Lesson Department

1. What are some of the important restraints suitable for children under twelve years of age?
2. What are the restraints most needed by teenagers, generally, but not needed by older children in the home?
3. Do mothers frequently wait to give admonition—restraining requests—until it is too late for the child to receive the blessing that obedience would have brought? Discuss.

As a Lamp We frequently fail to distinguish between the principle of the law and the regulations established to help us live the law. If we do not consider the basic purpose and principle of the law, we may develop unwarranted, prejudiced attitudes. We may resent the regulations, failing to see that they are as a lamp guiding us to the blessings which obedience would inevitably bring. We also forget that the basic truth of the law, being immutable, does not change although the rules and regulations may change from time to time according to our conditions and needs.

Illustrations from situations in this lesson

Example 1:
a. The basic law is "... Evidently the Lord's... People must be healthy and clean."
b. The code of dietary restraints was regulations intended to help ancient Israel remain healthy.

Example 2:
a. The basic law of health would apply to the Lord's people today as anciently.
b. The Word of Wisdom, which implements the Lord's law of health, is intended to help us today remain a healthy and intelligent people. The law of health and cleanliness has not changed but the regulation to secure obedience to the law has changed with the change in the manner of our living.

Implementation

1. Name some restraining laws which are useful and important to you, where the regulations are not now needed because of your increased knowledge.
2. When we were children we were forbidden to eat excessively of certain foods. This was the regulation of our household. The law of moderation is still important to me but, knowing the undesirable effects of excessive eating, I no longer need this household regulation.
3. Other examples of regulations follow:
a. When a man in a crowded bus, subway, or other similar situation gives up his seat to a mother carrying an infant, he is carrying out a socially enforced regulation in support of the law of consideration for womankind.
b. The code of desirable behavior taught to the Boy Scouts also illustrates the difference between regulations and such specific laws as: honesty, patriotism, kindness, health, first aid, survival.

An Important Factor

There is a difference between the operation of unchanging physical laws of the universe and the operation of intellectual and spiritual laws. In the latter, the human will is an important factor. The personal will of an individual may modify the exercise or penalty of the law. For example, let us say that the penalties for breaking the law of honesty are:
1. Loss of personal friends.
2. A guilty conscience.
3. Forfeiting the confidence of others.
4. The tangible effect of one's dishonesty upon those who suffer loss because of the dishonest action.

If, however, this person through personal will power repents and puts himself in a position to be forgiven, the penalty is modified much as the asbestos gloves may modify the burning effects of heat. Other examples may be suggested and discussed.

Conclusion

The concluding remarks of President Clark in lesson 7 of the Melchizedek Priesthood course of study are a fitting conclusion for this lesson: It is the first duty of... Latter-day Saints prayerfully to seek to know the law that they may render obedience to it, and next to learn the rule and regulation that they may diligently and reverently study them, that they may understand, know the reasons for, and live them. Many will be surprised to learn how understanding wipes out prejudice, resentment, and rebellion against those rules of life which make for righteousness.

A This much may be said with certainty and with no possible successful challenge: The people of the Church will search in vain for any law or rule or regulation of the Church, obedience to which will not make them better citizens, give them a richer cultural life, and bring to them a greater joy and peace; than they have ever before known; they will search in vain for law, rule, or regulation, obedience to which will bring any lasting shade of sorrow or tinge of regret. This cannot be said for much of the new "freedom" which too many unthinking have taken into their lives.

For Home Doing Help yourself to freedom by trying to live in harmony with the laws of life, both spiritually and physically.

title: Cultural Refinement—The Role of Education
author: Bruce B. Clark
content:
Ideals of Womanhood in Relation to Home and the Family
Lesson 2: The Role of Education
Dr. Bruce B. Clark
Textbook: Out of the Best Books, Volume 
Community Responsibility, Section Two
By Bruce B. Clark and Robert K. Thomas

"The object of true education is to make people not merely do the right things but enjoy them." — John Ruskin

Northern Hemisphere: Fourth Meeting, November 1969
Southern Hemisphere: April 1970

OBJECTIVE: To show that the development of individual capacity is a primary source of social or community strength.

ART "The Girl in White" by Julien Alden Weir (1832-1919)
American, Harris Fine Arts Center, Brigham Young University Collection, Provo, Utah. (June 1969 , page 448.)
Commentary on art selection by Floyd E. Brienholt, Chairman, Department of Art Brigham Young University. (June 1969 , page 449.)

A close study of "The Girl in White" provides a rewarding educational experience in that one learns about the personality and tastes of the artist. To the more casual observer, however, the picture represents an educational experience on another level—the experience of a lovely child involved in the beauty and wonders of nature.

"J. Alden Weir enjoyed the beautiful in life. It became an integral part of him. He also found a technique peculiar to him by which he could pass this on to us in the form of his paintings; a technique which harmonized with his personality and his special way of seeing. Such men of integrity and character play an important role in our education. As John Ruskin says, "The object of true education is to make people not merely do the right things but enjoy them.""

Throughout the entire history of the Church there has been an emphasis upon education. From the very beginning schools were established at all levels and Church members were encouraged to cultivate not only gospel knowledge and the skills of learning but also the cultural arts. The goal has always been to build in the hearts of Latter-day Saints an informed faith, an enlightened testimony.

Rather than being hostile to knowledge and the arts, the Church has encouraged and sponsored them—hence the rich program of cultural activities emphasizing drama, dance, music, literature, public speaking, etc., in the auxiliary organizations as well as the vast network of schools, seminaries, institutes, and colleges provided by the Church. Nor are these just modern developments. A study of the pioneer days of the Church will reveal that in spite of the struggle for food, clothing, and homes, priority was given to schools, theaters, and music halls, along with chapels and temples, for the pioneers understood that the mind and the spirit need food for growth just as vitally as does the physical body. Their westward-bound wagons were loaded with books and musical instruments as well as spinning wheels and blacksmith forges.

Many of the pioneers were self-educated, but most of them were nevertheless educated, even to the point of consciously cultivating skill in elocution, rhetoric, and penmanship. This is our pioneer heritage! No wonder we look back with admiration and a glow of pride, not only for the pioneers' great spiritual and physical courage as they triumphed over human persecution and the hostile, barren desert lands, but also for their love of the beautiful and the intellectually uplifting.

"..." Seek ye diligently and teach one another words of wisdom; yea, seek ye out of the best books words of wisdom; seek learning, even by study and also by faith." (D&C 88:118.)

Thus the Lord gave counsel through the Prophet Joseph Smith as the gospel was being restored, urging us above all else to study the scriptures but also to study other things. What other things? In Section 88 of the Doctrine and Covenants the Lord instructed us that we are to "... Teach one another... in all things that pertain unto the kingdom of God..." including, in his words, "... Things both in heaven and in the earth, and under the earth; things which have been, things which are, things which must shortly come to pass; things which are at home, things which are abroad; the wars and the perplexities of the nations, and the judgments which are on the land; and a knowledge also of countries and kingdoms." (D&C 88:77-79.)

In Section 90 he advised us to "... Become acquainted with all good books, and with languages, tongues, and people." (D&C 90:15.) In Section 93, we are counseled to "... Obtain a knowledge of history, and of countries, and of kingdoms, of laws of God and man, and all this for the salvation of Zion." (D&C 93:53.)

We are even promised that "Whatever principle of intelligence we attain unto in this life, it will rise with us in the resurrection," for "... If a person gains more knowledge and intelligence in this life through his diligence and obedience than another, he will have so much the advantage in the world to come." (D&C 130:18-19.)

Not only Joseph Smith as he was inspired of the Lord but also Brigham Young stressed the need for learning. Listen to the words of President Young:

All the knowledge, wisdom, power, and glory that have been bestowed upon the nations of the earth, from the days of Adam till now, must be gathered home to Zion. Every art and science known and studied by the children of men is comprised within the Gospel. See that your children are properly educated in the rudiments of their mother tongue, and then let them proceed to higher branches of learning; let them become more informed in every department of true and useful learning than their fathers are. When they have become well acquainted with their language, let them study other languages, and make themselves fully acquainted with the manners, customs, laws, governments, and literature of other nations, peoples, and tongues. Let them also learn all the truth pertaining to the arts and sciences, and how to apply the same to their temporal wants. Let them study things that are upon the earth, that are in the earth, and that are in the heavens.

(Discourses of Brigham Young, pp. 631-632)

245-46, and 252.) Now, as in the past, those we sustain as our living prophets encourage us to develop our talents and widen our intellectual and cultural horizons as evidenced not only by their encouragement of education but also by the breadth of their own reading. All of us know how abundantly President McKay and the other General Authorities enrich their talks and writings with passages drawn from the great literature of the world. Listen also as President McKay counsels us to harmonize learning with character: Lesson Department Wisdom is the right application of knowledge; and true education—the education for which the Church stands—is the application of knowledge to the development of a noble and Godlike character.... True education seeks to make men and women not only good mathematicians, proficient linguists, profound scientists, or brilliant literary lights, but also honest men combined with virtue, temperance, and brotherly love—men and women who prize truth, justice, wisdom, benevolence, and self-control as the choicest acquisitions of a successful life. (From Preface to booklet on L.D.S. Church Schools, 1968.) Our first responsibility as members of the Church is to study the scriptures and the words of the modern prophets for the fullness of the gospel that they contain. But beyond these we are invited to explore the best writings of the world for the supplemental knowledge and insight that these can give. At their best both religion and literature are concerned with building faith and affirming spiritual values and with opposing and exposing evil, worldliness, superficiality, and all things base and false. Space for this lesson allows us to print only three brief selections on education: Excerpts from "What Makes a Great Book?" by Mortimer Adler, "Cipher in the Snow" by Jean Mizer, and "He Ate and Drank the Precious Words" by Emily Dickinson. In the textbook five other selections are printed with questions and comments intended to stimulate thought and discussion about the proper goals of education and about techniques teachers can use to achieve those goals: (1) an essay by Samuel Scudder describing how famous 633 August 1969 Professor Louis Agassiz taught a great lesson about learning through observation, (2) some excerpts from the dialogues of Plato showing how the great Socrates provoked his students into thought by asking provocative questions, (3) a short story by Irwin Edman showing a modern Socrates in action, (4) some excerpts from the writings of the author identifying some of the goals and techniques of good teaching, especially for Latter-day Saint teachers, and (5) a humorous poem with a point by Leigh Hunt. "WHAT MAKES A GREAT BOOK?" BY MORTIMER J. ADLER In Volume 1, p. 19 of Out of the Best Books, we printed an essay by Mortimer Adler, a distinguished American educator. Because it is such an excellent statement on the power of great books to enlarge and elevate the mind we have decided to reprint it in Volume 5. The following are excerpts from this essay: Great books are those that contain the best materials on which the human mind can work in order to gain insight, understanding, and wisdom. Carl Van Doren once referred to great books as "the books that never have to be written again." They are the rare, perfect achievements of sustained excellence. The richness of great books shows itself in the many levels of meaning they contain. They lend themselves to a variety of interpretations. We need not read other books more than once to get all that they have to say. But we can always go deeper into great books. As sources of enlightenment, they are inexhaustible. Great books transcend the provincial limits of their origin. They remain as world literature, the ones men everywhere turn to again and again through the centuries. Mark Twain once remarked that "The great books are the books that everyone wishes he had read, but no one wants to read." Great books are not easy to read. No one should expect to understand them very well on a first reading, nor even to master them fully after many readings. I have often said that they are the books which are over everyone's head all of the time. That is why they must be read and reread. That is also why they are good for us. Only the things which are over our head can lift us up. "CIPHER IN THE SNOW" This dramatic real-life story was written by Jean E. Mizer, a teacher and counselor in Wood River High School, Hailey, Idaho. The story received the first-place award of $1000 in the National Education Association's 1964 Teachers' Writing Contest and was published in the NEA Journal for November 1964. According to the author the story is true but the names and location have been changed. Cipher in the Snow It started with tragedy on a biting cold February morning. I was driving behind the Milford Corners bus as I did most snowy mornings on my way to school. It veered and stopped short at the hotel, which it had no business doing, and I was annoyed as I had to come to an unexpected stop. A boy lurched out of the bus, reeled, stumbled, and collapsed on the snowbank at the curb. The bus driver and I reached him at the same moment. His thin, hollow face was white even against the snow. "He's dead," the driver whispered. It didn't register for a minute. I glanced quickly at the scared young faces staring down at us from the school bus. "A doctor! Quick! I'll phone from the house." "No use. I tell you he's dead." The driver looked down at the boy's still form. "He never even said he felt bad," he muttered, "just tapped me on the shoulder and said, real quiet, 'I'm sorry. I have to get off at the hotel.' That's all. Polite and apologizing like." At school, the giggling, shuffling morning noise quieted as the news went down the halls. I passed a huddle of girls. "Who was it? Who dropped dead on the way to school?" I heard one of them half-whisper. "Don't know his name; some kid from Milford Corners," was the reply. It was like that in the faculty room and the principal's office. "I'd appreciate your going out to tell the parents," the principal told me. "They haven't a phone and, anyway, somebody from school should go there in person. I'll cover your classes." "Why me?" I asked. "Wouldn't it be better if you did it?" "I didn't know the boy," the principal admitted levelly. "And in last year's sophomore personalities column I note that you were listed as his favorite teacher." I drove through the snow and cold down the bad canyon road to the Evans place and thought about the boy, Cliff Evans. His favorite teacher! I thought. He hasn't spoken two words to me in two years! I could see him in my mind's eye all right, sitting back there in the last seat in my afternoon literature class. He came in the room by himself and left by himself.

"Cliff Evans," I muttered to myself, "a boy who never talked." I thought a minute. "A boy who never smiled. I never saw him smile once." The big ranch kitchen was clean and warm. I blurted out my news somehow. Mrs. Evans reached blindly toward a chair. "He never said anything about bein' ailing." His stepfather snorted. "He ain't said nothin' about anything since I moved in here." Mrs. Evans pushed a pan to the back of the stove and began to untie her apron. "Now hold on," her husband snapped. "I got to have breakfast before I go to town. Nothin' we can do now anyway. If Cliff hadn't been so dumb, he'd have told us he didn't feel good." After school I sat in the office and stared bleakly at the records spread out before me. I was to close the file and write the obituary for the school paper. The almost bare sheets mocked the effort. Cliff Evans, white, never legally adopted by stepfather, five young half-brothers and sisters. These meager strands of information and the list of D grades were all the records had to offer. Cliff Evans had silently come in the school door in the mornings and gone out the school 635 August 1969 door in the evenings, and that was all. He had never belonged to a club. He had never played on a team. He had never held an office. As far as I could tell, he had never done one happy, noisy kid thing. He had never been anybody at all. How do you go about making a boy into a zero? The gradeschool records showed me. The first and second grade teachers' annotations read "sweet, shy child": "timid but eager." Then the third grade note had opened the attack. Some teacher had written in a good, firm hand, "Cliff won't talk. Uncooperative. Slow learner." The other academic sheep had followed with "dull"; "slow-witted"; "low I.Q." They became correct. The boy's I.Q. Score in the ninth grade was listed at 83. But his I.Q. In the third grade had been 106. The score didn't go under 100 until the seventh grade. Even shy, timid, sweet children have resilience. It takes time to break them. I stomped to the typewriter and wrote a savage report pointing out what education had done to Cliff Evans. I slapped a copy on the principal's desk and another in the sad, dog-eared file. I banged the typewriter and slammed the file and crashed the door shut, but I didn't feel much better. A little boy kept walking after me, a little boy with a peaked, pale face; a skinny body in faded jeans; and big eyes that had looked and searched for a long time and then had become veiled. I could guess how many times he'd been chosen last to play 636 sides in a game, how many whispered child conversations had excluded him, how many times he hadn't been asked. I could see and hear the faces and voices that said over and over, "You're dumb. You're dumb. You're a nothing, Cliff Evans." A child is a believing creature. Cliff undoubtedly believed them. Suddenly it seemed clear to me: When finally there was nothing left at all for Cliff Evans, he collapsed on a snowbank and went away. The doctor might list 'heart failure' as the cause of death, but that wouldn't change my mind. We couldn't find ten students in the school who had known Cliff well enough to attend the funeral as his friends. So the student body officers and a committee from the junior class went as a group to the church, being politely sad. I attended the services with them, and sat through it with a lump of cold lead in my chest and a big resolve growing through me. I've never forgotten Cliff Evans nor that resolve. He has been my challenge year after year, class after class. I look up and down the rows carefully each September at the unfamiliar faces. I look for veiled eyes or bodies scrouged into a seat in an alien world. "Look, kids," I say silently, "I may not do anything else for you this year, but not one of you is going to come out of here a nobody. I'll work or fight to the bitter end doing battle with society and the school board, but I won't have one of you coming out of here thinking yourself a zero." Most of the time—not always, but most of the time—I've succeeded. For Discussion 1. What is the meaning of the title "Cipher in the Snow"? 2. If the need to reach the lonely, insecure child is urgent in public school teaching, why is it equally urgent: (a) in our teaching within the Church? (b) to the woman in Relief Society? And (c) for the mother to reach the child in the home? "HE ATE AND DRANK THE PRECIOUS WORDS" We will give Emily Dickinson the final word on this lesson in education as she talks about the power of books to liberate and give wings to the human spirit. After all that other people can say in essay and in speech, the poet—the great poet—can often say it better and in fewer words as Emily Dickinson does here: He ate and drank the precious words, His spirit grew robust; He knew no more that he was poor, Nor that his frame was dust. He danced along the dingy days, And this bequest of wings Was but a book. What liberty A loosened spirit brings! These selections should aid us all in taking advantage of our opportunities for education. And always we have before us the example of the world's greatest teacher, Christ himself, who exemplified in both his life and his words the statement by Thomas Henry Huxley that the ultimate goal of education is "to learn what is true in order to do what is right."

title: Editorial: Turn to the Fathers
author: Vesta P. Crawford
content:
"And he shall plant in the hearts of the children the promises made to the fathers, and the hearts of the children shall turn to their fathers." (D&C 2:2.)

A well-loved poem speaks of the enduring influence and the continuing blessings that illuminate the world from the deeds and the words and the faithfulness of the fathers—"... For there was grandeur where the fathers stood."

When the hearts of the children turn to the fathers—even if the "children" are far beyond childhood—and even if the fathers still live upon the earth—there are memories and impressions and abiding direction which the fathers have given. Their strength, their leadership, their unselfish efforts, the blessings of their priesthood, are often vividly recalled.

And superimposed upon the children forever from the past are those deep and subtle influences which, over the years, still protect and bless them.

One woman still remembers and feels the sunlight and warmth of a summer day when she stood with her father in the doorway of an adobe ranch house, looking northward to a far expanse of wheatfields in a time of harvest. The father said, "There is your college education." The father then remarked that he knew little about the courses of studies offered in the universities, but he had heard that the learning and the development were good, and therefore necessary for his children. They would be sent to a university where spiritual enlightenment would be a lamp for the years to come.

His words were expressed in a moment of time, but the moment became forever in the memory of the daughter—love and sacrifice and strength and a plan for the future all joined together and made into a lasting commitment.

Another woman who was devoted to genealogical research and who found great joy in the patterns of the generations, wrote for her family and for the future families of her children the record of Volume 56 September 1969 Number 9—Belle S. Spafford, President Marianne C. Sharp, First Counselor Louise W. Madsen, Second Counselor Mayola R. Miltenberger, Acting Secretary-Treasurer

The fathers back along the years—the decisive events in their lives and the teachings that guided them "in every wilderness." The grandfather, who had lived as a boy in the river city of Nauvoo, had seen almost daily the Prophet Joseph Smith and had heard inspired doctrine and direction "in the grove."

The father of this grandfather, in the green hills of Vermont, had seen an early copy of The Book of Mormon, and had read it over and over by candlelight—the history of the long-ago fathers in the Western Hemisphere. From the book he learned much wisdom and instructed his family in the eternal verities of faith and righteousness.

Then he told them of his own ancestors in Plymouth, England, and how one of them was captain on "the ship of destiny" as he called the Mayflower, the ship that helped to part the waters for a journey to the promised land where the gospel would be restored.

The families of the fathers, reaching back into the early centuries, have become known to the families of the people who dwell in the 'fulness of times' and who look forward to a reunion of those related by the flesh and sealed together by the Spirit. It has been said in olden time and continues to this day ''... They judge not the fatherless'' (Isaiah 1:23), expressing the deprivation and limitations which those bereft in early years of an earthly father encounter. It is then that the written record of the father in his time gives comfort, and all the forebears stand as a legion for the years that one must walk with no visible father to smooth the way and offer protection. The arm of faith also is extended to the fatherless upon the earth, and the Father in heaven ''keepeth well'' the loved ones, for ''Have we not all one Father? Hath not one God created us?" As women of the Church, blessed with the heritage of our fathers, considering their counsel and the line of their authority, it is now a time for us to rejoice in the ''ordinances of heaven." 

Leone G.

title: In Memoriam—Mildred Bennion Eyring
content:
Mildred Bennion Eyring, May 23, 1896–June 25, 1969
Mildred Bennion Eyring, member of the General Board of Relief Society, passed away June 25, 1969 in Salt Lake City, Utah. Sister Eyring had served on the General Board for nineteen years, being appointed in 1950. While serving on the General Board Sister Eyring endeared herself to thousands of Relief Society sisters throughout the world. She was an excellent speaker, and devoted her talents to the needs of the General Board, giving service to education and other committees as well as to the work of conferences in the stakes. Sister Eyring was born in Granger, Utah. She was the daughter of Marcus and Lucy Smith Bennion. She married Dr. Henry Eyring; noted educator and physicist, August 25, 1928. They are the parents of three sons. There are six grandchildren. Sister Eyring was educated in the Granite School District in Salt Lake City. She was graduated from the University of Utah, where she headed the physical education department for women. She also did graduate work at the University of Wisconsin where she met her husband. She has been a member of the Sunday School General Board and has served loyally in ward organizations and sung in the choirs. She and her husband taught a Sunday School class which was especially appreciated and had a large attendance. At the time of her call to the General Board of Relief Society she was a counselor in the Monument Park Ward Relief Society. She was always devoted to Relief Society and has attended the meetings regularly, giving spiritual encouragement and the blessings of her inspirational companionship to all the women in the ward.

The General Board of Relief Society and members throughout the Church who knew and admired Sister Eyring extend heartfelt sympathy to her loved ones at her passing.

Farewell

How precious these last few days of hummingbirds and fields
To summer of tall corn; large cottonwood trees laden with leaves;
Sunflowers by the side of the road. All too soon the frogs and crickets
Are silent, and mothers are alone when the first school bell rings.

title: British Women Make a Union Jack Design in Knitting
content:
British Women Make A Union Jack Design In Knitting
Submitted by Norinne R. Callister, Former Supervisor, British Mission Relief Society

This unique "Union Jack" blanket was knitted by Sister Louisa Ridley (in the picture) and Sister Eda V. Longbone for Elder Dean Orton who took it home to his mother. It took the two women three weeks to make the blanket, working three to four hours a day, and lining the blanket took six hours. Sister Ridley and Sister Longbone belong to the Eastbourne Branch, Sussex, England. Joyce H. Walker is president of the branch Relief Society. Dorothy J. Roberts

title: The Universal Language
author: Ardean T. Loertscher
content:
Ardean T. W/m & ~ Loertscher

"TI'll get you some buckets," declared Angie, as she jumped up and ran into the garage. In a few seconds she reappeared with an armful of brightly colored tin buckets and shovels, and some tablespoons she had borrowed from the kitchen. The Japanese children grinned and giggled as they each received their allotted playthings from Angie.

One little girl, whom the others called Keiko, tenderly caressed Angie's bare white legs which emerged from the ruffled bottom of her pink sunsuit. The girl seemed to marvel at the skin that was at least two shades lighter than her own. Large mountains and valleys began to take form in the sand pile as the foursome repeatedly filled and emptied their buckets. A miniature car was buried and uncovered. Ribbons of roads, made by tractors, steam shovels, and trucks, wound around the mountains and through the valleys.

"Let's make a farm," cried Angie. "My uncle has a farm. I'll go get some cows."

The screen door banged behind her as she skipped into the house. She was greeted with "ohs" and "ahs" when she returned with a plastic bag full of horses, cows, chickens, ducks, pigs, sheep, dogs, and cats.

The girl named Keiko fingered a cow and repeated a strange word Angie couldn't understand.

"No, it's a cow," Angie corrected. But Keiko continued to say the strange word. Not being able to quite understand what this meant, Angie decided to build a field for the cows. She ran to the tall maple tree which stood at the end of the long back yard. There she picked up dry twigs and sticks. When she had all her chubby hands could carry, she returned to the sand pile.

One by one, she pushed the ends of the sticks into the sand next to each other. Her three playmates joined her in the task, and soon an area about a foot in diameter was encircled by the fence.

"The ducks need a pond to swim in," declared Angie as she ran for the sprinkler hose. She tugged the hose to the sand pile and carefully placed it in a ravine. Back she ran to the water tap on the side of the house.

As the water came on full force, the end of the hose flipped up, drenching the make-believe land in an unexpected shower. The three Japanese friends were also caught in the sudden sprinkle, but screams of delight and laughter escaped their mouths as the water cooled their skin.

Just then the sound of a car in the driveway drew their attention.

"That's my Daddy," revealed Angie. "He's a mission president."

Angie ran to greet the tall man who stepped out of the car.

"Daddy, come look at my farm," she coaxed.

"MY! You're a dirty, wet girl! You better come in the house and get cleaned up for dinner," replied her father.

"Who were your friends in the sand pile?" her father asked when they reached the bathroom.

"I don't know their names," Angie replied. "Can you understand them?"

"No, they talk funny," she declared rather puzzled.

"Isn't it hard to play if you can't understand them?" he continued.

Angie's face darkened for an instant but right back up again as she replied:

"But Daddy, we all laugh the same."

The leaves are scurrying across the street,

SCHOOL and little children run on new-clad feet. Swiftly the years are hurrying colorful, wind-spun.

Soon, the brittle leaves are gone, and school-days done.

title: With These Hands
author: Mary Jo McEwan
content:
The canvas of my day is yet empty.
And the pallet is clean.
My beloveds still slumber—
And God awaits my prayer.

I offer my hands this day unto their Creator,
To fashion a thing of beauty
From these virgin hours awaiting color and form.
Strike away the temptation to blotch and blurr, 
Lest they be lost—these priceless pearls of purity and purpose holy. 

Know ye not that thy place is at the side, and not below,
This companion to whom thou art given of the Lord?
Thou woman exalted, thou first to forgive and last to forsake,
Thou priestess, queen,
Thank thy God who made thee thus,
That thou wast born
A woman.

That the canvas may be vibrant and clean
With the work of loving hands.
Hands that will comfort, protect and perpetuate.
Hands that will discipline and adhere,
That the work may form in order and beauty.
Hands that will build, give, and guide,
Strengthening each fiber of importance,
And shunning the rest.

When the canvas is completed
In detail and deed,
May I hopefully seek my Lord again. 
Then, in humility and with thankfulness,
May I ask for his refreshment
And begin anew with these hands he has given me.
—Mary Jo McEwan
 
Fayola R.

Mrs. Mara K. Culp, twenty-seven, of Newport Beach, California, a small blond native of Riga, Latvia, who has lived in America the last twelve years, won the twenty-third annual Powder Puff Derby, women's cross-country air race, last July. She has two thousand flying hours to her credit and flew from San Diego to Washington in eleven hours and fifty-seven minutes.

Mrs. Sharon Sites Adams, whose husband is a sailing instructor, has become the first woman to sail alone the six thousand miles across the Pacific Ocean (from Yokohama, Japan, to San Diego, California). During the 75-day crossing in a 31-foot ketch, the 37-year-old housewife weathered severe gales, lost her rotator (which drags in the water and measures distance), to a shark and was becalmed for one week. Her radio transmitter became inoperative.

Letitia Baldridge has been social secretary to Ambassador and Mrs. David Bruce in Paris, to Ambassador Clare Boothe Luce, and to President and Mrs. John F. Kennedy. She became the first woman executive in Tiffany's history, and is currently public relations consultant for Chicago's Merchandise Mart, and for Sears, Roebuck. She is married and is a mother.

title: Social Relations—Work—a Road to Personal Fulfillment
author: Alberta H. Christensen
content:
Immortality and Eternal Life Lesson 
Work—a Road to Personal Fulfillment
Alberta H. Christensen

(Reference: Immortality and Eternal Life, Vol. 2, Selections from the Writings and Messages of President J. Reuben Clark, Jr., Melchizedek Priesthood Course of Study, 1969-70, Lesson 23.)

Northern Hemisphere: Third Meeting, January 1970
Southern Hemisphere: June 1970

To emphasize the value of work in meeting the requirements of happiness and of divine admonition.

Having been introduced to President J. Reuben Clark, Jr. (lesson 1, The Relief Society 786 Magazine, July 1967), we know of his general attitude toward work. We know that he believed in the value of work, that he was introduced to responsibility at an early age and that he liked to work. This lesson develops the idea that to work is following divine admonition, that it is a practical necessity, a privilege, and that it may become an enjoyable activity. The general theme applies to all—man, woman, and child.

Discussion in this lesson, however, will generally be focused toward work within the home and will focus upon the mother's specific position in introducing the children to one of the great challenges of life.

Do you remember the story of "The Little Red Hen"? One version narrates that:

One day the Little Red Hen was scratching in the farmyard when she found a grain of wheat. She asked, "Who will help me plant this wheat?" 
"Not I," said the duck.
"Not I," said the cat.
"Not I," said the dog.

"All right, I will do it myself," said the Little Red Hen. Then she asked successively:
"Who will help me cut the wheat?"
"Who will help me knead and bake the bread?"

To each of these questions she received the same negative reply. When the loaf was baked, savory, and brown, she then asked,
"Who will help me eat the bread?" 
"T will," said the duck.
"T will," said the cat.
"T will," said the dog.

The story concludes with this rhyme:
Lazy folks must hungry go
For they would not help me sow,
Neither would they help me reap
They had rather rest and sleep. 

All alone I baked the bread, 
Lazy folks should not be fed. October 1969

Question for Brief Discussion
How would you define work? An international dictionary defines work as "exertion or effort directed to produce or accomplish something." With this definition in mind, the duck, the cat, and the dog were justified in considering the tasks required to produce the loaf of bread as work. And in the story they did not want to work.

This story implies that eating the loaf was less effort or, at least, a more desirable activity. This nursery story obviously tries to teach children the idea that work is important and that each one should assume some responsibility in producing the good things of life.

What mother has not observed, with amazement, the amount of exuberant energy expended by her children playing jump-rope or the various running and hiding games, who, only a few moments earlier, said they were too tired to sweep out the garage or to wash the dishes?

Questions
1. What makes the difference?
2. How important is the mother's attitude in forming the children's reaction to "work" activities?
3. How will knowing that willingness to work and liking to work are characteristics of maturity help a mother teach her children to work?
4. How will this influence her own attitude toward what might otherwise be considered irksome, monotonous housework? 
How may a mother teach children to see that opportunities for service to others and for personal satisfactions are endless for those who are willing to work?

On 

Eden's gate was no sooner closed than man's earthly work began, for Adam was now introduced to the idea that he must work all the days of his earthly life. Among other things spoken by the Lord to Adam are these words:
By the sweat of thy face shalt thou eat bread, until thou shalt return unto the ground....(Moses 4:25.)

Since thorns and thistles were to be natural obstacles, the implication is that providing the necessities of life from the earth would not be an easy task. Nor has it been throughout the centuries.

In calling our attention to this statement of the Lord, President Clark states, "I believe that we are here to work, and I believe there is no escape from it." (Immortality and Eternal Life, Vol. 2, lesson 23.)

Being able to direct personal effort toward the achievement of definite goals other than to obtain food and shelter is a characteristic distinguishing the life of man from that of the lower animals.

The English poet Milton pays tribute to man and to work in the following poem from "Paradise Lost":
When Adam thus to Eve: 
"Fair consort, the hour
Of night, and all things now retire to rest,
Mind us of like repose; since God hath set
Labour and rest, as day and night, to men
Successive; and the timely dew of sleep,
Now falling with soft slumbrous weight, inclines
Our eyelids: long other creatures all day
Rove idle unemployed, and less need rest;
Man hath his daily work of body or of mind
Appointed, which declares his dignity,
And the regard of Heaven on all his ways;
While other animals inactive range,
And of their doings God takes no account.

Of the ways of men the Lord does take account and he has counseled us as to the spending of our precious days and years. A person who will not work, providing he is physically and mentally able to work, may rightly be termed an idler and the Lord has given, in our day, a direct commandment regarding idleness.

In the Doctrine and Covenants we read:
Now, I, the Lord, am not well pleased with the inhabitants of Zion, for there are idlers among them; and their children are also growing up in wickedness; they also seek not earnestly the riches of eternity, but their eyes are full of greediness. These things ought not to be, and must be done away from among them (D&C 68:31-32.)

How important is work to the world in which we live? How rewarding can it be to individuals and to groups?

President Clark speaks of work as indispensable to pioneer life and cites the struggles of the early members of the Church.

From lesson 23 of the 1969-70 Melchizedek Priesthood course of study, we quote from an address given in 1960:
A hundred years ago armed mobs plundered, burned, and murdered us.

I speak without heartburning or malice and with full charity. Three times we were driven by force from our homes, and all our property that we could not carry on our backs or in our wagons was taken from us. When ninety-five years ago our Prophet-leader was slain by an unrestrained local mob and we began our westward march over plain and mountain under that genius in leadership, Brigham Young, we moved under our own power, without subsidy, without loan, wished on our way only by the maledictions of those who drove us out from our homes and then appropriated, without payment for it, the property they forced us to leave behind. Arriving in the valleys of the mountains, we planted all our wheat, with enough flour to last only to harvest; our nearest food supply was one thousand miles away, reached only by ox teams; thousands of others were on the way to join us. A pest of locusts came, devouring everything on their way. Famine raised its gaunt frame. Then came the miracle of the gulls, the crops were saved, starvation was beaten back—a miracle as real as any that aided the ancient Israel in her escape from the bondage of Egypt. But even then we lived on short rations of the simplest foods, eked out by wild roots dug from the hillsides. So we struggled on against want and misery; toil and hardship were with us daily. But the Church survived; the people prospered. Character endured intact. We took care of our own poor. In times of scarcity neighbors helped one another. Time and time again, we passed through the fiery furnace; we came out of it each time, refined, with the dross burned away, re-inspirited, sanctified.

(Immortality and Eternal Life, Vol. 2, lesson 23.)

Experiences, in many ways similar, were encountered by other groups colonizing western America. In fact, pioneering life at all times has taxed the resourcefulness of the people and has required all individuals to work individually and cooperatively in order to survive. Out of their combined efforts, communities have been built, states and nations have evolved.

Class Participation: Consider pioneer conditions, briefly. Discuss the need and the willingness to work in order to survive in contrast to the trend toward idleness in times of plenty.

For the individual, work may be a door opening into fields more vast than the mind of man has yet envisioned. Though faith may be the great motivator, work is the spade of discovery, for it is work that makes the dream materialize. An intelligent person cannot work in any capacity without learning. To work is to discover. It is through individual effort and application that new vistas are glimpsed, horizons widened, and the ability to accomplish enlarged.

Examples of what work has produced and the possibilities of future accomplishment are breathtaking.

Class Participation: Discuss the following personal statements:

1. I stand in awe when I consider how much intelligent effort goes into producing even one of the innumerable commodities we use in the home, the school, and the shop, from simple electrical and manual appliances to such complex mechanisms as is found in the computer or the satellites that man has made to orbit into space.

2. The libraries of the world, taken collectively, house billions of books. They did not create themselves. They are the product of intellectual effort and of manual skill. I am grateful to all the individuals who have been involved in their production. I am a student, and as I view the stacks of books before me, I realize that books contain much of the best that man has thought and said. I know that ideas and facts contained in them can be mine only if I put forth personal effort to make them mine. At best, only a fraction of the knowledge contained will be transferred to me, a thought which spurs me to better use of my time.

3. The innumerable yards of fabric which one sees in a fabric store are evidence of work already accomplished. Without further labor they constitute mere yardage. With additional work of merchant, designer, and seamstress, the world may be clothed.

Glen C. Beeley, director of a well-recognized arts and craft institution, has some important things to say about work and a woman's emotional world. She believes that work, especially creative work, can and does perform miracles in the lives of unhappy, frustrated, and even physically handicapped women.

She states: I wish you could see the transformations I see in people. I believe that individuals cannot be truly happy unless they are constructively busy. Although some individuals are more inherently creative, everyone can be creative who is willing to work. Many people naturally find creative ways of expressing themselves and of usefully using their available time. Others, less naturally creative, need to be led to creativity, and thus, to happiness. They need to feel that they can do things with their hands. This assurance and this accomplishment lead to a degree of creative experimentation and of mental exploration. They think of new or different ways of making the same article.

But [concludes the director] it is not the articles of art or of usefulness that are really important, it is what creative work does for the individual. This is most rewarding. Individuals (including older people) should and do sense a personal growth from giving of themselves to others and to objectives. Thus they develop a fineness within themselves and a sensibility to the fineness in others. They learn to appreciate what others also are doing and can do. They need to feel, and they do, that they can, figuratively, "Sail their own ships."

The foregoing comments regarding arts and crafts, generally, refer to leisure time activity, although professional status and specific occupations may and sometimes are the result. Many women make special effort to enroll in such classes. They find personal satisfaction in creativity as do women who engage in the homemaking activities of Relief Society.

What may we say of the necessary, daily tasks of the homemaker? How useful is this work? How important is the attitude of the wife or mother in relation to this part of the world's work?

Many thought-evoking statements have been made regarding the need for and the important results of work. From the following statements, select for discussion as you desire and as time may permit:

Lesson Department: When I go into my garden with a spade, and dig a bed, I feel such an exhilaration and health that I discover that I have been defrauding myself all this time in letting others do for me what I should have done with my own hands.

—
