title: Cultural Refinement—The Problem of Communication
author: Robert K. Thomas
content:
Ideals of Womanhood in Relation to Home and the Family

Lesson 3 The Problem of Communication
Dr. Robert K. Thomas
Textbook: Out of the Best Books, Volume 5 Community Responsibility, Section Three By Bruce B. Clark and Robert K. Thomas
There is no more sure tie between friends than when they are united in their objects and wishes.
Cicero

Northern Hemisphere: Fourth Meeting, January 1970
Southern Hemisphere: May 1970
OBJECTIVE: To show that those who can communicate can work together, and those who can work together make civilization possible.

ART "Advice to a Young Artist" By Honoré Daumier (1808-1879)
French, National Gallery of Art, Washington, D. C., Gift of Duncan Phillips.
Painting reproduced in the June 1969 , page 447.
Commentary on art selection by Floyd E. Breinholt, Chairman, Department of Art, Brigham Young University.

Honore Daumier at the age of seven decided to be an artist. To gain background for his chosen field, he frequented the poorer quarters of Paris and haunted the corridors of the Louvre. He studied Rembrandt and Michelangelo, nature, and the manners and customs of his period. Although he is better known as a lithographer and political cartoonist, it has been said that he was one of the greatest painters of the 19th century.
Daumier expressed very deep feelings in a spirit of satire and ridicule. His caricatures are without hatred or spite, although they are biting. There is a direct simplicity in the expressions of what he saw and felt. All of his work has social significance. His art was and still is a very direct means of communication of ideas, a universal language.
Artists today use art for propaganda purposes just as Daumier did in his time. The painting, "Advice to a Young Artist," is perhaps more mild than many of his works. This is not a portrait of any two particular people but more a generalization of a typical situation in the world of work. We can all learn from others who go before us, especially if they are willing to share their knowledge with us.
In this very commonplace idea, expressed in a simple, earthy manner, one senses a certain kinship between the artist and the common working man, a certain reality that makes the commonplace beautiful when stripped to the bare essentials. None of his works is pretty. All tinsel and glitter have been left out and only simple truth remains. Perhaps this is why many have placed Daumier in the school of realism.
All of his works, full of compassion for the downtrodden, are attempts at communicating the unvarnished truth as he saw it. As in all great art, Daumier in his work gives us not merely a photographic likeness of his subject but an interpretation of it in which he communicates what he knows, thinks, and feels.
 
From childhood, many of us have heard that "sticks and stones may break my bones but names can never hurt me." A far more accurate assessment of reality is given in the Apocrypha when we are reminded that "the stroke of the whip maketh marks in the flesh, but the stroke of the tongue breaketh the bones." Most physical pain is less excruciating than having someone we love and respect speak harshly to us. To feel unloved or unwanted is often more crippling than physical impairment.
Perhaps modern research rightly emphasizes the role of communication in establishing positive human relationships. Such communication need not be in words—how rarely are our deepest feelings easily translated in speech—but we must use words to discuss non-verbal communication, so the importance of language in communication cannot be minimized.
To concede this, however, is not to admit that the basic problem in poor communication is failure to convey information clearly. If this is assumed, we focus our attention so totally on methods of communication that we risk losing ends in means. Although it has been popular to assert recently that the medium we use is an integral part of the message we are trying to convey, it probably needs to be said also that an insignificant message belittles any medium that carries it.
The word communication originally suggested sharing or making common an idea or an experience. If the idea we want to share can touch a positive chord in another's background, the chances for successful communication are immeasurably increased. It is when we try to get through to those who do not share our ideals and values that we have extreme difficulty. It is probably not very accurate to speak of a "communication problem" under such circumstances, lest we assume that we need to refine our method rather than examine our beliefs.
The selections we have chosen for this lesson make less effort to examine procedures than to assess results.

In the beginning poem "The Secret Heart" by Robert P. Tristram Coffin the father is hardly conscious of how deeply he is revealing his love to a half-awake child. But the youngster's identification with his father is so total that he has no difficulty in interpreting the look on his parent's face.
As the next to the last couplet suggests, the moment of illumination was not long, but it was enough to light "all about." The result of such a reassuring insight has been a lifetime of happy memories which are symbolized by a brief vision of his father's "secret heart."
The tone of this tender poem helps us understand how prepared the boy was for his revelation. The real impact of this moment depends very little on the situation, even though it yields a symbol that is memorable one.

October 1969

Question for Discussion
Why is unqualified devotion difficult to hide?
Comment on some of the less obvious ways in which we communicate our identification with that which we love.
Another April
In "Another April" by Jesse Stuart we touch briefly on a number of communicative levels. The boy understands his childlike grandfather as only a child could. If his youthful candor is disconcerting, he sees without the restraints which experience imposes, and he reacts freely and openly, if also superficially.

In the relationships of mother and child there are hints that the young boy understands far more than his simple conversation would suggest and that the mother senses this. Her relationship to her father is now a mildly ambiguous one: she must be both mother and daughter. She doesn't let her practical need to care for him destroy her sensitivity to what he has been to her and still is. The grandfather's relationship with the natural world he had seemed to control totally as a young man has become more passive, but possibly more discriminating. In the terrapin, the old man has a natural counterpart, a checkpoint in his renewed intercourse with the world which is the more precious because it can no longer be taken for granted.
 
Question for Discussion
The grandfather seems to have established meaningful relationships with everyone and everything in this story. Why has he been so successful?

In this short essay Oliver Wendell Holmes helps us see that "full" communication may not be "good" communication. If we try to share sacred feelings with those who are not prepared to respect them, we may lend ourselves to grievous exploitation. A man or a woman is often as vulnerable as an open wound to a marriage partner. In our awareness of the totality of relationship that a good marriage demands, we must also be aware that there is no other human relationship that should ask so much. Letting down restraints in an attempt to understand associates is neither an automatic route to self-enlightenment or a guarantee of better inter-personal relationships. Limits of taste and self-respect should inhibit the intimacies we share. Confession is not a shortcut to that communication which is earned by trust, rooted in similar commitment, and justified by joint responsibility.

Question for Discussion
Why are we tempted to give keys to our "side-doors'" to those who might not use them appropriately?

In his popular version of a careful research study, "Can You Get Along With Your In-Laws?" Edward Fales provides persuasive evidence that all communication problems with in-laws appear to yield to loving acceptance by all concerned. The lists of "do's" and "don'ts" he provides are obviously the result of much experience, but, as he himself suggests, the "one greatest lesson" in the Cornell survey he is reporting on is that people who refused to think of their relatives as "in-laws" but only as "members of our family" were able to establish warm, happy relationships with them.

Question for Discussion
How can we prepare our own sons and daughters to realize that, in marrying, they marry a family as well as a person?

The letters exchanged by Mark Twain and his wife, Livy, are difficult to match in all literature for tender concern and sensitivity to one another. Each almost aches with the desire to understand and help. Mark found it difficult to believe, and Livy was often embarrassed by his lack of taste. She helped him on both counts, some modern critics notwithstanding. Mark aided a gently spoiled child, who took herself very seriously, turn into a resourceful, stimulating woman. In these remarkable letters there is not only growth but a truly notable desire on the part of both to match that ideal that each has of the other.

It is comforting to see how effective words, lovingly chosen, can be in encouraging personal development and fostering marital harmony. Excerpts from two letters follow:

My dear, dear Livy:
November 28, 1868

I do love, love, love you, Livy! My whole being is permeated, is renewed, is leavened with this love, and with every breath I draw its noble influence makes of me a better man. And I shall yet be worthy of your priceless love, Livy. It is the glad task of my life—it is the purest ambition and the most exalted that ever I have known, and [I shall never, never swerve from the path it has marked out for me.

Mark had early found that Livy was careless in her spelling. Up to this point he hadn't teased her about it, but he can't resist in talking to her about his sister:

My sister is a good woman, familiar with grief, though bearing it bravely & giving no sign upon the surface; & she is kind-hearted, void of folly or vanity, perfectly unacquainted with deceit or dissimulation, diffident about her own faults, & slow to discover those of others. She isn't such a gem as you; (by a long shot) & neither is any other woman, but then she is a very, very excellent woman anyway. You'll like her, Livy—she don't seem to spell worth a cent.

You see she spells cow with a K. And she has spelled "tripped" with only one p, & she puts only one t in "delighted," & only two s's in "expression," I can stand those little blunders well enough, but I do hate to see anybody spell John with a G. I look upon that as perfectly awful. I notice that you always spell John with a G.—Now forgive me, Livy, darling—you know I wouldn't poke fun at you to save a man's life if I thought it would wound you. I don't care a straw how you spell, Livy dear—I hardly ever notice when you make a mistake—and bless you I am just as proud of you as if you could 795 October 1869 beat the Unabridged dictionary spelling. It would be a pity indeed if I presumed to criticize your spelling—I who am sown as thick with faults as you are with merits, & shining virtues, & beautiful traits of character; & yet you have found it in your heart to take me just as I am & lift me up & bless me with your priceless love—I never can be your critic, my loved and honored Livy.

Question for Discussion
How much do you think the repeated assertions of love by Mark Twain and his wife actually contributed to that love?

With "Neighbor Rosicky" we conclude our discussion of communication on a highly encouraging note. As the ideals and values of the characters in this fine story come together, effective communication flourishes. Although, Anton Rosicky and his wife, Mary, have worked out most of their adjustments before this account begins, the series of flashbacks, by which part of this story is told, lets us see some of the circumstances that aided their developing unity. It is important to note that years of marriage have not blunted the Rosickys' sensitivity to each other's whims: Anton caters to his wife's sweet tooth, and she has something hot ready for him when he returns from town. Real marital harmony, however, is not built on indulgence. Shared principle is at the base of the Rosickys' strength. Perhaps this is best exemplified in the scene near the end of section two. As the creamery agent tries to persuade them to sell him their cream, Mrs.

Rosicky declares emphatically that she would rather "put some color into my children's faces than put money into the bank." The agent merely turns to Mr. Rosicky to get the real decision, knowing that it is usually the husband's word that really counts among the newly emigrated families with whom he does business. "I guess we'll do like she says," said Rosicky.

The developing relationship of Polly to her father-in-law is possibly the key to this story. Here we have concentrated many of the forces that are supposed to block real communication: Polly is not a recent immigrant; Rosicky is. Rosicky is old; Polly is young. Polly is gregarious, Rosicky is not. Polly is a daughter-in-law.

It takes a rather dramatic crisis to reveal the depth of Rosicky's understanding of and love for Polly and her own "tender heart." In the few minutes that Rosicky and his daughter-in-law share during his heart attack, all barriers that had seemed to inhibit communication between them are swept away. In realizing how much her father-in-law loves her, Polly tells him of her expected child before letting anyone else know. In finding out how tender and capable Polly is, Rosicky sees through the facade of sophistication she had erected between them and knows that she will be the kind of wife Rudolph needs.

Question for Discussion: Why is Rosicky so effective in bringing out the best in everyone he meets?

Like to do grape juice—but hate the mess?

title: In Memoriam—Elder John Longden
content:
Elder John Longden, Assistant to the Council of the Twelve, passed away August 30, 1969, in Salt Lake City, Utah. He had served as an Assistant to the Twelve since 1951. During that time he fulfilled many important callings for the Church and endeared himself to members throughout the world.

Elder Longden was born in Oldham, England, on November 4, 1898, the son of Thomas J. and Lizetta Taylor Longden. The family emigrated to the United States and Salt Lake City ten years later. Elder Longden was graduated from LDS Business College and attended the University of Utah. He served as a missionary in the Central States Mission.

Elder Longden had a successful business career. At the time of his retirement, he was area manager for National Electric Products Corporation. Interested in music and dramatic arts, Elder Longden studied voice and dramatics. He was well-known throughout the area as a gifted singer. He gave freely of his talent, singing at Church services, weddings, funerals, and other gatherings.

In 1924, Elder Longden married LaRue Carr in the Salt Lake Temple. They had three daughters: Mrs. Grant (Gail) Hickman, and Mrs. Loren C. (Sharon) Dunn. The first daughter, Helen Margaret, died in childhood. There are three grandsons and five granddaughters.

Elder Longden was always devoted to the gospel and served in several MIA superintendencies, as bishop, and as high councilman in two stakes, and on the General Church Welfare Committee prior to his call as a General Authority. As a General Authority, he served on the General Priesthood Committee and several youth committees. At the time of his death, he was supervising all stakes and missions in the British Isles. Previously, he had supervised missions in the South Pacific, Australia, and New Zealand.

The General Board and Relief Society members in all stakes and missions of the Church extend heartfelt sympathy to the family of Elder Longden and to his many friends. He has left a heritage in faith and dedication to the gospel for others to follow.

title: Report and Official Instructions
author: Belle S. Spafford
content:
President Belle S. Spafford [Delivered at the Officers Meeting of the Relief Society Annual General Conference, October 1, 1969]

Once again it becomes my duty to present to you a brief report of the status of Relief Society, together with a few General Board instructions and recommendations.

The latest available figures are those from the 1967-68 annual report, since the 1968-69 reports were not due in the General Board Office until September 20 and, of course, are not audited and compiled as yet. In order to make the information somewhat more current, growth figures for the previous year will be used in estimating present conditions. Precise figures will be available to you when the compiled report for the fiscal year is published in a forthcoming issue of The .

We mourn the loss, by death, of two valiant and eminently capable members: Evon W. Peterson, General Secretary-Treasurer, and Mildred B. Eyring.

With the growth of the Church, the number of stake and mission Relief Society organizations has grown. There were as 816 of September 1968, 462 stake and 74 mission Relief Society organizations, with 328 mission districts and more than 6,500 ward and branch Societies.

For the past several years there has been consistent growth in membership, but there still remain far too many Latter-day Saint women not enrolled who need Relief Society. We, therefore, appeal to officers and members alike to intensify their efforts in enrolling new members. The membership appeals are almost endless. There are, in reality, few women who cannot be reached by one or the other of these.

Estimating the membership growth for 1968-69 to be the same as for the previous fiscal year (or 11,000), there is now a total membership of 322,500. Of this number 3,500 are non-Latter-day Saints.

Each year we receive gratifying reports of non-Latter-day Saint Relief Society members becoming baptized members of the Church.

It is our conviction that there are comparatively few enrolled members who do not attend some meetings during the year. We must recognize that under certain circumstances a sister may be unable to attend regardless of her desire to do so; for example, personal or family illness may interfere, or the infirmities of age or the hazards of winter roads where distances to the meeting place require car travel. Nonetheless, attendance is a continuing concern and ward presidents should be urged to do all in their power to interest women in regular attendance and to help make it possible for them to attend.

Second session Relief Societies are a boon to countless women, as are well-managed nurseries for mothers with young children.

The General Board requests that great emphasis be placed upon the compassionate service program which is fundamental in the accomplishment of the purposes of the Society and which, according to the reports, seems to be somewhat on a plateau insofar as volume is concerned. Today's troubled, costly, and insecure world, with its measure of tragedy in the lives of many faithful sisters and good families, calls upon us deeply to understand our responsibilities in this area, and to respond to needs with readiness, competency, and love.

It is our opinion that days care of the sick, which declined by more than 5,000 eight-hour days in 1967-68, have not taken any significant upturn during the 1968-69 year. We suggest that you report and official instructions exercise watchcare so that Relief Society will not falter where this service is needed in the home.

Figures on visits to the sick also have remained somewhat constant—between 395,000 and 400,000—as have the number of hours of other compassionate services, which were approximately 750,000 hours. Let us be sensitive to all such needs.

We particularly call your attention to the desirability of Relief Society presidencies calling on a family at the time of a death and making available to it, if desired, the services of the Society in connection with the funeral. We estimate that 1,000 bodies were dressed for burial during the 1968-69 year, and assistance including the providing of food was given in connection with more than 12,000 funerals. Such service is usually welcomed and is comforting and helpful.

At times, our sisters need to be reminded of the value of going the second mile in rendering voluntary humanitarian service as individuals—on their own.

I call attention to an important program which is proving of value in our Latter-day Saint hospitals. I refer to the hospital Volunteer Auxiliary program, sometimes referred to as the "Pink Lady" program. We encourage sisters who have time, health, and an aptitude for service in a hospital setting, where there is a variety of work situations, to contact your local Latter-day Saint hospitals. Full information will be readily given you.

We commend the stakes who have participated in the Red Cross Home Nurse Training programs. Approximately 5,426 women have completed this course, made available to them through the cooperative efforts of the American Red Cross and the Relief Society.

Relief Society sisters consistently support the Church Welfare Program with ward presidents having made approximately 100,000 family visits under the direction. Of their respective bishops, and 94,000 other contacts during the past year. Relief Society members contributed during the year 756,000 hours on Church Welfare projects.

It is estimated that there were close to 5 million visits made to homes during the 1968-69 fiscal year by more than 148,000 visiting teachers. We increasingly see their value as an aid to the bishop in ascertaining cases of temporal need among worthy Latter-day Saint families and also as an aid to him through the Home Teachers in reactivating inactive members.

Visiting teachers are a great asset to Relief Society itself in bringing women who are enrolled into membership and activity. The visiting teaching program has value in fellowshiping new converts to the Church, most of whom soon make excellent visiting teachers themselves, thus enlarging our reservoir of teachers.

Let us aim to enlarge our visiting teaching program and continually improve the quality of the visit and attendance at the visiting teacher meeting, as well as the quality of the confidential report to the ward president.

The spiritual living lessons for the forthcoming season conclude the course based on the Doctrine and Covenants. It is expected that by the time this course is concluded in May 1970, members will have thoughtfully read the entire Doctrine and Covenants in order that they may enjoy fully the spirit of this book of scripture, increase their knowledge and understanding of the revealed truths contained in the revelations, and be guided by them in their day-to-day activities.

Visiting teaching messages correlate with the Family Home Evening Manual and are designed to renew our faith and guide us as we meet life from day to day. The social relations lessons for the coming year are based again upon the writings of the late President J. Reuben Clark, Jr., and are correlated with the Melchizedek Priesthood course of study.

The cultural refinement course will stress the individual and human values as seen through literature, art, and music, widening the scope by emphasizing a woman's responsibilities in community life and the world in which she lives.

LESSONS FOR THE USE OF LAMANITE WARDS AND BRANCHES The General Board is pleased to announce that special lessons for Lamanite Relief Societies are now ready for distribution. This new set of Lamanite lessons has been prepared so as to fit into any standard three-ring binder. The set is wrapped in cellophane and includes sheets of visual aids following each lesson. The cost for a set is $3 postpaid. They are to be ordered from the office of the General Board.

Exclusive of the homemaking discussions that are prepared by the General Board, activities for the homemaking meeting are planned by the local Relief Societies with the General Board setting the purposes of the meeting and giving general guidelines. It is the rule of the stake and mission district board to suggest, display, and demonstrate suitable items which might be produced or demonstrations which appropriately might be conducted. This year's estimate of number of items produced is in excess of 114 million. This was over and above the countless recorded homemaking demonstrations and other helpful homemaking activities.

We are delighted to report the increasing interest and general improvement in the music program. We now have over 3,600 local Singing Mothers choruses, with 52,500 singers participating. Special concerts are being held with a high degree of success.
Results of the Song Contest were excellent. There were 223 entries. Both the first and second award-winning numbers will be heard during this conference—the first award number in the General Session, and the second in this morning's session. These numbers will be published and Report and Official Instructions will be available for purchase at the music stores. We are indebted to our former board member, Sister Cleone Eccles, for the inspiration and the financial means which led to the establishment of the contest.

It is with gratitude that we acknowledge the tireless and competent service of more than 7,000 sisters whose callings as Magazine representatives are so important to the organization and to the sisters as individuals. The present circulation is 285,000 (7,000 Spanish). In November 1957, the subscription price was set at $2. Through rigid editing and publishing economies, and by virtue of the labors of the sisters in obtaining subscriptions, we have been able to maintain the $2 subscription price for twelve years. It is with genuine regret that, regardless of our efforts to continue the $2 subscription price, it has become necessary to increase it to $2.50 for the twelve issues per year. This price change becomes effective January 1, and applies to all subscriptions, including renewals, which start with the January 1970 issue of the Magazine or any month thereafter. Subscriptions beginning with September, October, November, or December 1969 issues will be at the $2 price. Subscriptions for one year at a time only will be accepted. (There will be no change at this time in the subscription price of the Spanish edition.)

Presidents, we urge you fully to explain this action to ward and branch presidencies and enlist their support in making certain that the number of subscriptions does not decline. We suggest that occasionally the president take a few moments of time in the regular meeting to call attention to some special feature of the Magazine, thus creating interest in it and deepened appreciation for it.

Presidents, will you kindly request ward presidents to instruct their Magazine representatives not to send currency through the mail in payment for Magazine subscriptions. Payments are to be made by check or Post Office money order in the interest of safety. Some orders have been received with currency missing. We remind you that General Board regulations state that in sending currency, you do so at your own risk.

One of the original purposes of the Magazine was "to serve as an outlet for the literary aspirations of Latter-day Saint women." It has met this purpose well. Publication of the award-winning poems and short stories in the Poetry and Short Story Contests, respectively, has brought interest and enrichment to the publication. This year there were 469 entries in the Poetry Contest and eighty-six in the Short Story Contest. We call your attention to the excellence of these award-winning works, published in the April 1969 Magazine.

We are pleased to announce that by action of the General Board there will be an increase in the amount of cash award paid to the writers of the award-winning entries.

In attendance at this conference are a number of choice Lamanite Relief Society leaders, to whom we extend a special welcome. Relief Society must do its part with far greater insight, understanding and intelligent effort than heretofore. The influence of Relief Society can be a potent one in helping the Lamanite mother to lift the standards in her home and to make her home a center where the gospel is taught and lived—a place of order, attractiveness, and peace.

The Lord continues to bless Relief Society in meeting its assignment in the Garment Distribution program. We acknowledge with appreciation the spirit and efficiency with which this program is being carried forward.
We request stakes in close proximity to one another which are conducting single stake operations to consider coming together as multiple stake operations. These are proving much more satisfactory to patrons and are a more efficient plan of distribution.

An outgoing president is advised, in turning over the records to her successor, to see that all outstanding financial obligations of the Society incurred during her administration are met. In those instances in which a reorganization is anticipated, outgoing presidents are advised not to proceed with any new plans or projects which involve unusual expenditures of Relief Society funds.

title: Sequoia Soil or Desert Sand
author: Alda Larson Brown
content:
Alda Larson Brown

Now when Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judaea... There came wise men from the east... Saying... We have seen his star... And are come to worship him. (Matthew 2:1-2.)

To the wise men of the east, whose search took them over mountains and deserts to the hills of Judaea, the star was a sign of the birth of Jesus, but they were looking also for a greater brightness, a more glorious illumination.

With the Savior's birth, the plan of salvation was given again to the earth "for it pleased the Father that in him should all fulness dwell." We, too, in our time and place, have seen the star, but have we participated in its full radiance? To each and all of us there comes a spiritual search for an application in our own lives of the message of Christmas. Beset with many interests and urgent responsibilities, some of us who know the meaning of the star may have difficulty in realizing its enduring splendor. In a multiplicity of demands, we dwell among seemingly urgent details—how shall the home be decorated? We ask. How can we provide for all the names on the Christmas lists? Have we reciprocated all the greeting cards? All the pleasing elements of our observance have their place in providing joy and warmth and beauty—in uniting the family on a memorable occasion. And yet the transcendent meaning of Christmas is neither gain nor giving of material objects.

title: These Stones, My Poems
author: Vilate R. McAllister
content:
I am as a rushing stream Flowing over these rough stones, my poems. From far places where I have been— From the rugged mountains of learning, From the desert of maturing insight, From the forest of deep appreciation, From the valleys of experience, I have gathered abrasive sand and silt, Carrying all along with me. Each time that I, so laden, Touch one of these my poems, Its roughest corners fall away, And it takes on an encouraging bit of polish. Possibly, if I continue long enough, Some of these will become smooth, round pebbles, Shining and beautiful— Such as children delight to find—and keep! Violate R. McAllister

title: Trouble and More Trouble
author: Kathleen Peace Gregory
content:
Kathleen Peace Gregory Dewsbury, Yorkshire, England
m
"Oh, Steve, it's perfect." Jean Roberts clasped her hands excitedly as her eyes took in every detail of the picturesque cottage. The weather-worn stone, the small latticed windows framed with climbing ivy, and the cowled chimney sitting slightly askew upon the low roof.
" Dad, Mum, can we explore?" "Let's go in. Quick." "I'll bet a witch once lived there." "Boys, simmer down, it's not going to disappear." Their father laughed at the excited faces in the back of the car. "We have the key," he continued, "and we can go and look at the cottage, but that dog stays put."
"That dog" breathed heavily and a long pink tongue reached out for Steve's ear. "No, Trub, you can't get out, you would only get into mischief." Steven spoke sternly.
The dog gave a resigned sigh as they stepped from the car, and laying his head on his paws, gazed with mournful eyes at the departing family.
Jean opened the gate, and the three older boys hustled through, nearly bowling each other over. Steve, following, carrying baby James, laughed. "There's plenty of garden. They will certainly enjoy living here if we decide to buy," he said. "My mind is nearly made up," replied Jean, picturing crisp white net behind the latticed panes.
"There may be dry rot, woodworm, and medieval plumbing," Steve warned her. The path to the front door was overgrown with weeds, and the once trim lawns were scattered with daisies and buttercups, while a few pansies grew harmoniously with the wild clover.
Steve approached the door with the key as the boys came bounding back. "Dad, there's a tree big enough to hold a swing," John, the eldest of the three, gasped, his eyes bright with anticipation. "We could have a pet rabbit, couldn't we?" asked Pete, the younger one, while Martin, whose physique belied his appetite, said, "And there're some apple trees and blackberry bushes. Oh, boy!" Jean and Steve laughed as he went on, "We might even be able to grow our own peas." "And you'd go nibbling them all," John poked him with a grubby finger.
"Come on, chaps, let's see what's inside."
Inside the low-ceilinged house, old oak beams ran the length of the downstairs rooms. These lovely old beams are thirsty for polish, Jean thought, and my brass ornaments will look most attractive. Chintz covers and drapes I have furnished and redecorated already! Reluctantly she brought her thoughts back to the present.
A large lounge led to an even larger stone-floored kitchen, and a narrow stairway reached to the upper floor. There they found three bedrooms, one being slightly larger than the other two. A small room under the eaves reached by a wall ladder completed the layout of the cottage.
"Well?" Steve asked inquiringly, "what do you think of it? Would you be happy here? It will mean making new friends Trouble and More Trouble and neighbours," he went on, "but that shouldn't be too difficult."
"A small town will be a big change from the city," Jean replied, "but I think the garden itself is well worth moving for. I know the boys will enjoy living here, and I'm sure Trub will like it, too," she added.
"Trub had been with them for nearly a year. He had followed one of the boys home, a longhaired, mournful-eyed dog of doubtful parentage, and after making the usual inquiries and finding no owner, the dog had become part of the family. The boys had wanted to name him Butch, but after the escapades of the first few weeks, with Steve repeating, 'That dog is nothing but trouble,' they decided to call him Trouble, shortened to Trub.
"Well that's that." Steve surveyed the ring of expectant faces as they all converged in the tiny hall. "I will inquire about a mortgage and, after seeing about a few alterations and modernization, we will move in as soon as we can. In a year or two we can maybe build on an extra couple of rooms."
"We could sleep on the grass in a tent," Pete volunteered hopefully, 'and Trub would guard us from the witch." "What about during the winter when there's snow up to the roof?" Martin asked.
"Oh, boys, you do exaggerate." Jean laughed. "Now, we will work it out so that we all have a decent roof over our heads. Come on, let's go back to the car and see what Trub is doing."
They filed out of the cottage, Jean locking the door behind them. "Oh, Mum, just look at Trub," John giggled as they approached the car. The dog had his wet nose pressed flat to the window, his tongue dripping against the glass.
"Just look at that window," groaned Steve. "He's healthy, that's why his nose is wet," Pete informed them knowingly. "My Primary teacher says so, and she knows everything."
"Well I wish he would be healthy somewhere else," his father answered, hustling the boys into the rear seat of the car. "Will one of you clean the window with this cloth?" he added, helping Jean and young James into the front seat ready for the return journey.
"I don't think that this house will ever be tidy again." Jean ruefully surveyed the general chaos as the removal men took their departure. "And whose bright idea was it to let the boys help?"
Steve straightened his back wearily and with a twinkle in his eyes he replied, "My dear, moving house is an adventure and they are making memories today."

By this time next year we shall all be looking back and saying 'Do you remember when the bottom dropped out of the toy box as Martin carried it upstairs and we found that 'all the old toys which we had thrown away had been rescued, and how Pete curled up and fell asleep in a drawer, and how John got stuck in a doorway trying to move a wardrobe?" "Okay. You've convinced me,' Jean laughed, "but I'm _ very glad you agreed to let Sister Garner take care of young James and the dog. I dread to think what could have happened with those two helping!"' 5 "They were just about to set to work when a resounding bang shook the house. "The boys!" they both spoke together as they hurried to investigate. Entering the largest bedroom, they found what seemed to be a heap of struggling arms and legs, as three red-faced, giggling boys emerged from the chaotic muddle of the bed. "What on earth are you doing now?" Jean inquired. "I thought the ceiling was coming down." "I put your bed together again,' John, the eldest of the boys, replied, looking at his parents. "And then we all jumped on it to test it and it collapsed," he ended lamely. "I should think it did collapse." His father looked ruefully at the melee. "Come on all together, let's get it built up again. I don't relish the thought of sleeping on the floor tonight." "When you're finished here will you come and help me set out the crockery, please, boys?" Jean asked. "There are two packing cases to empty before we can have any supper." "Sure thing, Mum," Martin replied, "and can I have some peanut butter sandwiches, please, to keep me going until suppertime? I'm starving." "That child must have an abyss for a stomach." Steve shook his head. "He ought to be like a plum dumpling, but he never gets any fatter. Off you go, boys. I'll see to this while you help your mother." Gradually the bright summer colors deepened to the mellow tints of autumn, and the family settled down in their new home. Trub had been wildly excited when introduced to his new home. He had run around inspecting every tree in sight, hurtling through the house like a miniature tornado, skidding on the polished floors. Finally he had settled in an untidy heap on the hearth rug, his exploration completed. The older boys had quickly made new friends, while Jean's bright disposition came to good use, and she was soon on friendly terms with the occupants of nearly every house on the road. One person, however, always answered her kindly greeting with a grunt and never smiled. An upright, elderly gentleman, the retired Colonel Watkins, lived further along the road, and seemed very brusque. Jean often wondered about him, living alone and so unfriendly. He didn't even have a smile for young James in his pram, until one day when Trub took a turn in the events. "Mum!" Martin came rushing into the kitchen. "Mum, I've been to see Colonel Watkins, and I think he's ill and he was angry." "You've been to see Colonel Watkins?" Jean was astounded. "Yes. You see Trub escaped," Martin began, "and I went looking for him and I went to the colonel's to ask him if I could look around his garden and he did look ill,' Martin ended breathlessly. "Well, why was he angry?" Jean inquired. "I hope you weren't rude?" "Oh, no,' Martin answered firmly, "he asked me why I wanted to look in his garden, and I answered and said very politely, 'I'm just looking for Trouble,' and he said that I was a naughty impur imp Se "Impertinent," Jean finished for him. "Yes. Impertinent little boy, and he told me to go home," Martin went on, "and then he began to cough and he looked real bad." "Oh, dear,' Jean sighed, not knowing whether to laugh or cry. "I will have to go and explain, and if the poor man is ill, I'll see if he needs any help." "T'll play with James while you go," offered Martin. "Thank you, dear." Jean hurried out and down the road 829 November 1969 to the home of Colonel Watkins. He answered her knock, looking just as ill as Martin had said, his military bearing gone and his blue eyes clouded. After making apologies and explanations on Martin's behalf, Jean went on, "I wondered if I could get you anything? My son said that you are ill." "No, thank you. I can manage," he answered gruffly. "Well maybe I could send you some lunch over with one of my boys?" Jean asked hesitantly, "or some hot lemon? You look as if you have caught flu." "Oh, you might as well come in," he said grudgingly, holding the door open for her, and followed her in to sink into a deep chair by the low fire. "Must apologize," he grunted. "My daily woman is ill. Tried to do a bit but had to give up. Flu, as you said." Jean took off her coat and began to tidy up, softened as she glanced at the old man. He seemed more human today, all the fire gone out of him. "I must go now," she roused him gently. "I'll send some broth over. Try to take some, it will make you feel better, and I'll call around later." Next morning John and Martin accompanied Jean to visit the colonel. As John cleaned out the old-fashioned hearth, Martin chopped sticks outside to light the fire. Jean tidied up and dusted while the old man watched. "T had a son," he said suddenly. Jean turned as John looked up. "Is he grown up now, Sir?" he asked. 830 her eyes ' "No," answered the colonel sadly, "he was killed during the last war. He was only twenty-two, and the shock made my wife so ill she died not long after. He was our only child." "Oh, my dear, I am sorry." Jean went and knelt by the chair, suddenly understanding the colonel's sternness and apparent dislike of children. He was nursing a broken heart. "What he must have suffered, living alone all these years," Jean told Steve that evening, "gradually being left all alone with his grief. I'm not surprised he became grim and hard." Within a few days all the family were visiting the colonel, including Trub, who, most of the time, liked to lie with just the tip of his nose upon the old man's slipper. The colonel was much better and seemed to enjoy watching the boys help tidy up. "T'm sorry Trub wandered into your garden and made a nuisance of himself." Martin said one morning, as the colonel was watching the tidy-up operation. "You know he isn't really a bad dog, Sir," Martin continued earnestly, "he only needs - understanding." "T'm sure that's all he needs," replied the old man, trying to keep a straight face. "You see he wasn't really our dog," Martin went on. "He just came one day and stayed.

He didn't have anyone to love him so we adopted him." The colonel laughed. "I'm sure you never could have bought such a handsome looking creature. He would have been far too expensive."

"Mum, I have an idea," John turned suddenly to his mother. "Why don't we adopt Colonel Watkins as our Grandpa, if he will let us? We have only one and he lives a long way from us, and the colonel is all alone."

They all looked at the old man inquiringly. Suddenly he had turned rather red. His cold had come back it appeared as he blew hard into his handkerchief.

"And you will be our very own Grandpa, and tell us stories?" Peter's voice was shrill with delight. "Oh, boy!"

Trub, hearing the excited chatter, came bounding into the room to investigate, skidded to a halt by the colonel's chair, and laid a large paw on his knee.

"It seems that even Trub agrees," laughed the old man with misty eyes. "Good old Trub, for without him, we might never have become friendly. With four mischievous boys and a delinquent dog, I'm certainly going to have my work cut out."

"Yes," he continued more seriously. "I would like to be your Grandpa and I'll never forget the honor you have paid me today. I only wish my dear wife was here beside me to have a second chance at happiness."

"That's settled, then," Jean said briskly, with a slight sniff. "Come on, chaps, there's work to be done and Grandpa's fire is burning low." They all laughed, and with a contented sigh Colonel Watkins settled further down into his chair with Trub stretched out at his feet.

title: Winged Return
author: Leora Larsen
content:
| sent her out to shake a rug
When she was six and three:
She watched a bird with wings of wind
And never quite came back to me.

One day, full-grown, she went away
With love strong for her nest.
Still, when the days and duties weigh,
She flies to me for rest.

Leora Larsen

@ Today is the most wonderful day of your life!
Today is the day that you can do marvelous things!
We cannot change what happened yesterday. We do not know what tomorrow will bring, but today is a gift to us from Heavenly Father! Let us make the most of this gift.
Today we can teach our children and make them happy.
Today we can be real neighbors and worthwhile friends.
Today we can study and learn to do new things.
We must stop regretting the things that happened yesterday. We cannot change any part of the day that is gone, yet, how many of us there are, who, like Lot's wife, turn to pillars of salt when we look back.
In our case the pillars of salt are salt tears, whether they are shed actually or whether we weep inwardly in our regret. All the tears in the world will not change the past, yet if we should spend the energy and the thought and the pain that we spend on vain regret in making the present all that we can make it, what a happy future ours would be!
Stop regretting.
If you are regretting lost opportunities of learning, start learning now. If you are mourning about something you did not accomplish, start working and planning something you can accomplish now.

title: Art Gallery
author: Dorothy R. Cox
content:
Art treasures are here along this quiet street as in the corridors of a great gallery. A still life "Old Man in the Sun," picture of serenity. A landscape "Autumn Gold" depicts a weeping birch dripping bright tears. Lovers hand in hand "Portrait of Young Love." Even I, framed in this moment of time, title "Walk Along a Country Lane."

Art treasures are here, painted in living colors, signed by the Master.

Dorothy R. Cox

Her most recent undertakings have been making miniature and regular sized hats for tourists. She has averaged making twenty quilts a year for more than twenty-five years. Her family of eleven children, thirty-six grandchildren, and twenty-eight great-grandchildren have received many items of clothing and quilts from her. She is a generous and faithful Relief Society member, and still is an active visiting teacher who is welcomed in all homes because of her lovely spirit and happy outlook on life.

Ina Wilson Hummell, St. George Stake, Utah, has filled the lives and homes of those close to her with many lovely handmade articles, ranging from exquisite quilts to delightful children's toys. She has a talent for painting, and makes the faces of dolls and other toys truly come to life. Sister Hummel is the mother of five children and there are nine grandchildren to add to her joy. All of them are appreciative of her talents, but more so of her wonderful spirit and the things she teaches them.

title: On Doors Closing and Opening
author: Mabel Jones Gabbott
content:
A new door does not always open wide
Immediately when the familiar door clicks shut;
and so one stands lost, inside
the unfamiliar loneness till the pricks
of shutters on the retina adjust
and shadows form in new dimensions like or unlike what is gone.

The question: must
one hurdle forward, seek another dike
to satisfy this need that one be used,
or stand and wait, slowly gathering strength
until reason rises, unconfused
and in perspective; and we learn at length
that doors within the mind must open first
before the heart accepts a new sunburst.

title: Cultural Refinement—The Place of Entertainment
author: Bruce B. Clark
content:
Ideals of Womanhood in Relation to Home and the Family
Lesson 4—The Place of Entertainment
Dr. Bruce B. Clark
Textbook: Out of the Best Books, Volume 5 Community Responsibility, Section Four by Bruce B. Clark and Robert K. Thomas

"'Any pleasure which keeps the heart from God will be fatal to the soul.' —Richard Fuller"

Northern Hemisphere: Fourth Meeting, February 1970
Southern Hemisphere: June 1970

OBJECTIVE; To show that there is a valid place for entertainment and pleasure in life but that these should always be in good taste and in accordance with Church standards.

MUSIC The Telephone, a "mini-opera" by Gian Carlo Menotti (1911- born) American (iItalian)

Commentary on musical selection by Clawson Cannon, Assistant Dean of College of Fine Arts, Brigham Young University. 

The Webster New World Dictionary states that the word "entertain implies planned amusement or diversion, often with some intellectual appeal." For the past thousand years in the western world, many musical compositions have been written specifically to entertain. Among these especially composed to this end is the opera, introduced in the culturally and musically sophisticated courts of Italy at the turn of the seventeenth century. Certainly the little opera by Gian Carlo Menotti, The Telephone, is an excellent vehicle for providing a few minutes of entertainment to all who hear it. This little opera should appeal to those whose activities are being interrupted by the ringing of the telephone, usually for the most frivolous matters.

There is a long tradition among some churches, going back through centuries of medieval Christianity, which assumes that God is not pleased when people enjoy themselves with the pleasures of this world.

Under such a philosophy money is regarded as evil, love between men and women is thought of as tainted with sin, art and recreation are looked upon with suspicion. November 1969 Through centuries of history some churches have banned dancing and drama as forbidden evils, some have legislated that their religious leaders must not marry, and some have prescribed heavy, drab clothing as the only dress suitable to express religious devotion. Philosophically a whole intellectual movement known as asceticism flourished for centuries advocating strict self-denial as a means of spiritual self-discipline. Severe "mortifications of the flesh" were practiced, apparently under the supposition that God is somehow pleased with human self-punishment. This is not, however, the position of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. The Church believes that God wants people to be happy and enjoy themselves, in this life as well as in the life to come. "Men are, that they might have joy" (2 Ne. 2:25) is one of the great principles expressed in The Book of Mormon. Money sometimes does promote evil but need not do so if used for good purposes. Love between men and women sanctified by marriage provides genuine happiness. Art and recreation when in good taste abundantly enrich life and are in harmony with the fulness of the gospel plan. The key words in the preceding sentence are "when in good taste." From the beginning The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has not only permitted but actually sponsored extensive programs in music, drama, dance, literature, and athletics. Always, however, emphasis has been upon encouraging refinement, wholesomeness, and good taste. Discuss the term "good taste." What does it include? What are the limitations? Reading, as millions of readers know, provides an inexhaustible and wonderfully varied storehouse of entertainment. In addition to all of the aesthetic values of literature and all its rich insights into people, ideas, and problems we should never forget that one of its great and valid functions is to entertain. We live in a world of pressures and problems. How wonderful that we can draw upon literature for the relaxation and delight it provides in addition to its many other values! From Aristophanes in ancient Greek times to writers such as E. B. White, James Thurber, and Al Capp in our own century, every era has had its great humorists. Thousands of delightful stories, novels, poems, dramas, and essays are available to illustrate the rich entertainment literature can provide. In the five preceding years, and this year, too, we have studied "serious" literature concerned with problems, values, ideals, etc. Now in this one lesson we want to emphasize the power of literature to entertain. The lesson consists of the following four selections: (1) Stephen Vincent Benét's short story "The Devil and Daniel Webster," (2) Ring Lardner's short story "Haircut," (3) "Baker's Blue-Jay Yarn" as excerpted from A Tramp Abroad by Mark Twain, and (4) a discussion of and brief excerpts from the essays of Charles Lamb. Since each of the four selections is too long to print in this Magazine, they will need to be studied from the textbook. All four selections are also available in many libraries throughout the world if readers do not have access to the textbook. "THE DEVIL AND DANIEL WEBSTER" This, the longest of the four selections, is an excellent example of literature at its entertaining best—delightful, compelling, clever, yet also very wholesome, and rich with meaning. Cleverness of some kind can be found in almost every sentence, making the story great fun when read silently and even greater fun when read aloud. Why was the story written? First, to eulogize Daniel Webster, making him emerge out of the pages of history as a vivid folkhero of America—courageous, dynamic, idealistic, eloquent, and bigger-than-life in the Paul Bunyan tradition. Second, to glorify the American ideal of freedom and human dignity—the beautiful dream of America. Third, simply to delight the reader, to entertain as literature and literature alone can do. Some works of literature live primarily through their message. Some live primarily through their power to entertain. When we have important meaning combined with wonderful entertainment, as in this story, then we have a great story. Because "The Devil and Daniel Webster" illustrates so excellently this combination of delightful entertainment and wholesome meaning it has been chosen as the feature selection in this lesson on the place of entertainment in our lives. When discussing the Lesson Department story the class leader should emphasize both of these aspects. The beautiful section of seriousness near the end when Daniel Webster talks about ideals of freedom and about the dignity and glory of mankind is one of the finest passages Benét ever wrote. Note, for example, this paragraph describing Daniel Webster's speech to the jury in defense of Jabez Stone: He started off in a low voice, though you could hear every word. They say he could call on the harps of the blessed when he chose. And this was just as simple and easy as a man could talk. But he didn't start out by condemning or reviling. He was talking about the things that make a country a country and a man a man. And he began with the simple things that everybody's known and felt—the freshness of a fine morning when you're young, and the taste of food when you're hungry, and the new day that's every day when you're a child. He took them up and he turned them in his hands. They were good things for any man. But without freedom they sickened. And when he talked of those enslaved, and the sorrows of slavery, his voice got like a big bell. He talked of the early days of America and the men who had made those days. It wasn't a spreadeagle speech, but he made you see it. He admitted all the wrong that had ever been done. But he showed how, out of the wrong and the right, the suffering and the starvations, something new had come. And everybody had played a part in it, even the traitors. The next two paragraphs are also beautifully meaningful. Then the story returns for the last three pages to a lighter tone and one of the most interesting endings to be found in the literature of America. "HAIRCUT" This is included in this lesson for two reasons: first, simply because it is such an excellent story, and second, because it shows how fun can become evil if it is had at the expense of other people. Although the story is written very cleverly, providing amusement all the way through, most readers will realize that underneath the entertainment the author has a serious purpose: to show how twisted and cruel humor can be when it causes other people to suffer. "Haircut" is the story of a barber who talks in an uninterrupted stream of words all the time he is whacking off the hair of a stranger who has come to the barber shop.

The barber talks mostly of a man in the town named Jim Kendall who recently was killed in an hunting "accident." Although the barber repeatedly refers to Jim as a man with a great sense of humor, just bubbling over with the fun of playing practical jokes on other people, we gradually realize that Jim's sense of humor is terribly perverted. He laughs loudly at the embarrassment and suffering of other people, especially when he can cause the embarrassment and suffering himself—such things as sending anonymous postal cards to men asking them where their wives were when they were out of town the other day or sending a feebleminded boy to get a left-handed monkey wrench. The man is really twisted and cruel instead of funny in his sense of "humor." And the barber is hardly better. Although perhaps not quite so cruel as Jim, the barber apparently is even more stupid because he doesn't even comprehend all that is going on. In fact, one of the best things about the story "Haircut" as a work of art is that it is written so skillfully that the barber tells a story he doesn't understand in such a way that the reader fully understands. As a story "Haircut" is clever and entertaining, however, humor is never really humor if it must be had at the cost of ridicule or injury to someone else. As our children learn in Primary, "Fun is not fun unless it's fun for everyone." And in adult words, "Entertainment that is not uplifting can turn thoughtless indulgence into real degradation."

This delightful excerpt from Mark Twain's A Tramp Abroad is also included mostly as an illustration of the wholesome entertainment that literature can give. Mark Twain, one of our great masters of style and humor, describes animals that can talk. Perhaps space will permit just a couple of paragraphs as samples of his unique style and humor:

There's more to a blue-jay than any other creature. He has got more moods and more different kinds of feelings than other creatures; and, mind you, whatever a blue-jay feels, he can put into language. And no mere commonplace language, either, but rattling, out-and-out book-talk—just bristling with metaphor, too—just bristling! And as for command of language—why, you never see a blue-jay stuck for a word. No man ever did. They just boil out of him!

And another thing: I've noticed a good deal, and there's no bird, or cow, or anything that uses as good grammar as a blue-jay. You may say a cat uses good grammar. Well, a cat does—but you let a cat get excited, once; you let a cat get to pulling fur with another cat on a shed, nights, and you'll hear grammar that will give you the lockjaw. Ignorant people think it's the noise which fighting cats make that is so aggravating, but it ain't so; it's the sickening grammar they use.

Now I've never heard a jay use bad grammar but very seldom; and when they do, they are as ashamed as a human; they shut right down and leave. You may call a jay a bird. Well, so he is, in a measure—because he's got feathers on him, and don't belong to no church, perhaps—but otherwise he is just as much a human as you be.

And I'll tell you for why. A jay's gifts, and instincts, and feelings, and interests, cover the whole ground. A jay hasn't got any more principle than a Congressman. A jay will lie, a jay will steal, a jay will deceive, a jay will betray; and four times out of five, a jay will go back on his solemnest promise. The sacredness of an obligation is a thing which you can't cram into no blue-jay's head.

Now, on top of all this, there's another thing: a jay can outswear any gentleman in the mines. You think a cat can swear. Well, a cat can; but you give a blue-jay a subject that calls for his reserve powers, and where is your cat? Don't talk to me—I know too much about this thing.

And there's yet another thing; in the one little particular of scolding—just good, clean, out-and-out scolding—a blue-jay can lay over anything, human or divine. Yes, sir, a jay is everything that a man is. A jay can cry, a jay can laugh, a jay can feel shame, a jay can reason and plan and discuss, a jay likes gossip and scandal, a jay has got a sense of humor, a jay knows when he is an ass just as well as you do—maybe better. If a jay ain't human, he better take in his sign that's all.

Although space does not permit the printing of a complete essay by Lamb it would be unfair to end this lesson on entertainment without making at least a few comments on the writer who is probably the most delightful essayist English letters has ever produced—Charles Lamb (1775-1834). For all who will take the time to discover Lamb with his inimitable style and ineffable charm, hours of leisurely, delightful reading are guaranteed. In spite of a good deal of tragedy in his personal life and of drudgery in his professional life, Lamb had a sense of humor that bubbled up in all that he said and wrote. As he plays up and down the whole scale of humor we feel his humanity, his genial warmth, the endless charm of his prose style, and the range of his keen, curious mind.

Although he has a great deal to say on a wonderful variety of subjects his chief aim is to entertain, not to instruct or persuade. And entertain he surely does, captivating readers with the magic charm of his matchless style in almost every paragraph that he wrote. Sometimes in his essays he is serious. For example, in "Dream Children" he lovingly talks about the children he yearns for but never had. In "Old China," one of the loveliest of his essays, he reminisces about his youth when possessions were scanty but joys were spontaneous and dreams were free. And in "Modern Gallantry" he regrets that men tend to be more gallant with young or wealthy women than with women who are homely or poor or old. Men should treat all women with the same gallantry, he feels—just because they are women and men are their protectors.

As a brief sample of Lamb's exquisite style, we print the opening lines of "A Chapter on Ears":

I have no ear. Mistake me not reader, nor imagine that I am by nature destitute of those exterior twin appendages, hanging ornaments, and (architecturally speaking) handsome volutes to the human capital.

Better my mother had never borne me. I am, I think, rather delicately than copiously provided with those conduits; and I feel no disposition to envy the mule for his plenty, or the mole for her exactness, in those ingenious labyrinthine inlets—those indispensable side-intelligencers...

When therefore I say that I have no ear, you will understand me to mean—for music.

CONCLUSION In the foregoing discussion and selections it is the objective of this lesson to point out that entertainment in the form of good, wholesome humor has a valid place in our lives. While the very word "humor" has meant different things to different centuries, it is never destructive or malicious. As Louis Untermeyer has written, "It does not fly to assault the mind, but laughs its way into the heart. Humor is healing, wholesome, recreative, and rejuvenating." (From "A 'Treasury of Laughter," selected and edited by Louis Untermeyer.)

ERRATUM In the Cultural Refinement Lesson 1—The Meaning of Freedom, July , page 556, Woodrow Wilson is listed as the twenty-seventh President of the United States. Woodrow Wilson was the twenty-eighth President.
